message.content:
Here is a classic proof by induction (one can also use a telescoping argument; see footnote).  We want to show for all integers n≥1:  
 1³ + 2³ + ⋯ + n³  =  [1 + 2 + ⋯ + n]²  =  [n(n + 1)/2]².

**1. Base case (n=1).**  
LHS = 1³ = 1.  
RHS = [1·(1+1)/2]² = [1·2/2]² = 1² = 1.  
So the formula holds at n=1.

**2. Inductive step.**  
Assume the formula holds for some n≥1, i.e. assume  
 1³ + 2³ + ⋯ + n³ = [n(n+1)/2]².  
We must prove it then holds for n+1, namely  
 1³ + 2³ + ⋯ + n³ + (n+1)³ = [(n+1)(n+2)/2]².

Starting from the LHS at n+1 we write  
 1³ + ⋯ + n³ + (n+1)³  
= (1³ + ⋯ + n³) + (n+1)³  
⟶ by the induction hypothesis  
= [n(n+1)/2]²   +   (n+1)³.  

Factor out (n+1)² from these two terms:  
 [n(n+1)/2]² + (n+1)³  
= (n+1)² · [ n²/4  +  (n+1) ]  
(because [n(n+1)/2]² = (n+1)²·n²/4).  

Combine the bracket:  
 n²/4  +  (n+1)  
= n²/4  +  (4n+4)/4  
= (n² + 4n + 4)/4  
= (n+2)²/4.  

Hence  
 (1³ + ⋯ + n³ + (n+1)³)  
= (n+1)² · [(n+2)²/4]  
= [(n+1)(n+2)/2]²,  

which is exactly the desired formula at n+1.  This completes the induction.  
Therefore by induction the identity holds for all n≥1.

∎

Footnote (telescoping proof).  Observe  
 a_k := [k(k+1)/2]².  
Then  
 a_k – a_{k–1}  
= [k(k+1)/2]² – [(k–1)k/2]²  
= (1/4){ k²[(k+1)² – (k–1)²] }  
= (1/4){ k²[ (k²+2k+1) – (k²–2k+1) ] }  
= (1/4){ k²·4k }  
= k³.  
Therefore  
 ∑_{k=1}^n k³ = ∑_{k=1}^n (a_k–a_{k–1}) = a_n – a_0 = [n(n+1)/2]².
